chunk_summary: Moderate acute malnutrition (MAM) involves infection-driven catabolism and gut dysfunction, including environmental enteric dysfunction (EED), which impairs recovery. Management requires energy supplementation (100–130 kcal/kg/day) and addressing infection, food insecurity, and caregiving constraints.
learning_objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of wasting, including infection-driven catabolism and gut dysfunction.
Recognize environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) and its impact on nutrient absorption and recovery.
Learn WHO guidelines for energy supplementation in children with moderate wasting (100–130 kcal/kg/day).
Identify strategies to prevent relapse, including addressing infection burden and food insecurity.
key_facts: Acute wasting results from a mismatch between intake/absorption and physiological needs, exacerbated by infections.
Environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) causes intestinal inflammation, impaired absorption, and barrier disruption.
WHO recommends 100–130 kcal/kg/day for children aged 6–59 months during recovery from moderate wasting.
Persistent poor appetite is a clinical marker of unresolved illness or inflammation, not behavioral issues.
Relapse after discharge often reflects unresolved infection burden, food insecurity, or caregiving constraints.
reference_quote: Environmental enteric dysfunction: an overview — 'EED is a subclinical enteropathy linked to repeated enteric pathogen exposure, causing intestinal inflammation and impaired absorption.'
document_key_topics: Environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) and gut health
Energy supplementation and relapse prevention in MAM
